text: Welcome to another installment of robotic inguinal hernia surgery. Today, we'll review a case performed in a woman who presented with an inguinal hernia and possible femoral hernia. This was a sixty-five year old woman presenting with a left groin bulge. She had a history of hypertension, multiple pelvic procedures, had a reducible inguinal hernia, no symptoms on the contralateral side. Before our encounter, she had already had an ultrasound with a possible femoral identified.

The surgeon begins the operation by developing a peritoneal flap, visible from the transabdominal view. The indirect hernia and femoral area weakness are observed. The surgeon carefully maintains a preperitoneal flap, avoiding the pre-transversalis layer, and initiates dissection in the visceral compartment. The intermediate fascia is taken down, transitioning into the visceral compartment, with fatty and areolar tissue being reflected towards the lateral sidewall.

The surgeon reduces a small indirect hernia by anatomically orienting and pulling the hernia sac out of the canal, ensuring no other tissue is displaced. The intermediate fascia is used to protect against injury to small branches of the epigastric vessels while dissecting around the retropubic space to safeguard the bladder. The surgeon identifies and reduces a small fat-containing femoral hernia, preparing a flat area for mesh placement.

The surgeon develops a lateral pocket and identifies the round ligament, which is transected using energy to prevent bleeding from small vessels. The transection is performed close to the peritoneum to avoid nerve injury near the internal ring. Further retropubic dissection is conducted, ensuring at least two centimeters of contralateral dissection, while tidying up the surgical field.

The surgeon prepares to place mid-weight polypropylene mesh over the myopectomial orifice, addressing a small femoral defect and indirect hernia. A single stitch is placed into Cooper's ligament and another onto the lacunar ligament for fixation. The flap closure is performed from lateral to medial to minimize tension, with insufflation pressure reduced to between six and eight.
